nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-moving blackwater streams, floodplain pools, and leaf-litter zones of the Amazon and Orinoco basins; heavily vegetated shaded areas with accumulations of dead leaves and submerged wood

decorPreferences: deep leaf litter on the substrate (Indian almond/catappa leaves strongly recommended)
dense planting with broad-leaved plants (Amazon swords, Anubias, floating plants)
driftwood and branches for cover and tannin release
dim or subdued lighting — floating plants to diffuse overhead light
dark substrate to simulate blackwater habitat
no strong current — still or very gentle flow only

diet.notes: Strict piscivore in practice. Feeds almost exclusively on live feeder fish (small livebearers, tetras, danios sized to fit its highly protrusible jaws), live ghost shrimp, and live insects (crickets, waxworms, mealworms). Rarely accepts frozen foods even after extensive conditioning; prepared foods are almost universally refused. A consistent live-feeder supply is mandatory for long-term maintenance. Gut-loaded or vitamin-enriched feeder fish are recommended to improve nutritional value.

goodWith: similarly-sized non-fish tankmates in a species-only setup (rarely practised)
kept singly in a dedicated species-only aquarium
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be eaten (will be consumed without hesitation)
fast-moving active fish that stress the leaf fish or disrupt its ambush posture
cichlids and other aggressive fish that may bully it

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Monocirrhus polyacanthus showing full lateral leaf-mimic profile. Check FishBase image page for current attribution.

summary: The Amazon Leaf Fish is one of aquaria’s most remarkable leaf mimics: its laterally-compressed brown body, chin barbel, and drifting locomotion are indistinguishable from a floating dead leaf. An ambush predator that engulfs prey with a lightning-fast jaw strike. Specialist-only: live feeders are essential and prepared foods are almost never accepted.
careNotes:
Monocirrhus polyacanthus is the quintessential leaf-mimic fish of the Amazon basin. Its laterally-flattened body, mottled brown-tan coloration, prominent chin barbel (mimicking a leaf petiole), and slow drift-and-hover locomotion combine to create a nearly perfect imitation of a dead floating leaf. This disguise is both a camouflage strategy and an active hunting posture — the fish drifts passively toward prey fish until within striking range, then deploys its remarkably protrusible jaws to engulf victims in a fraction of a second.

Water chemistry is critical: this is a true blackwater species requiring soft, acidic, tannin-stained water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–8). Peat filtration, catappa (Indian almond) leaves, and driftwood are strongly recommended to maintain appropriate water conditions and provide cover. Tap water in most regions must be conditioned with RO or rainwater to achieve acceptable softness. Temperature should be kept warm and stable at 24–29°C.

The diet constraint is the defining challenge of this species. Monocirrhus polyacanthus is essentially a live-feeders-only fish in aquarium practice. Small live fish (appropriately-sized guppies, endlers, small tetras, or danios), live ghost shrimp, and live insects are the staple diet. A very small number of hobbyists have reported success transitioning specimens to frozen foods over extended periods, but this is the exception; the vast majority of fish will starve rather than accept non-living food. A reliable supply chain for live feeders must be established before acquiring this species.

A species-only setup of at least 80 L is recommended. No tankmates are appropriate: all fish small enough to fit in its mouth will be eaten, and active or aggressive fish will disrupt the leaf fish’s calm, and induce chronic stress. Lighting must be subdued; floating plants and leaf litter replicate the natural shaded, debris-rich environment. Filtration should provide gentle turnover without strong current. Water changes should use pre-conditioned soft acidic water to avoid parameter shock.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has been achieved but is uncommon. A conditioned pair should be given their own dedicated tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.5–6.5, very low hardness), abundant leaf litter, and stable warm temperatures. Eggs are typically deposited on flat surfaces such as broad leaves or submerged wood and are guarded by the male. Fry are tiny at hatching and require appropriately-sized live foods (Artemia nauplii, micro-worms, paramecia) from the outset. Fry are fragile and water quality must remain pristine. Breeding is rated difficult due to the requirement for live-only diet conditioning of parents, exacting water chemistry, and the challenge of rearing tiny fry on live micro-foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Monocirrhus polyacanthus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (10 cm), water parameters (pH 5.0–7.0, temperature 24–29°C, very soft water), native range (Amazon and Orinoco basins), blackwater biotope requirements, diet (live fish, invertebrates; rarely accepts frozen), behaviour, tank setup, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Monocirrhus polyacanthus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Polycentridae, Perciformes), adult size (to 10 cm TL), native range countries (Brazil, Peru, Colombia, Venezuela, Guyana), habitat description, and diet classification (carnivore/piscivore).

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Amazon Leaf Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist care guide. Confirmed live-feeder-only diet, leaf-mimic behaviour description, blackwater/soft acid water requirements, minimum tank size recommendation, and specialist-level care rating. Also referenced chin barbel as leaf petiole mimic.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Amazon Leaffish species profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist publication. Confirmed ambush predator strategy, protrusible jaw mechanism, leaf-drift locomotion, and the near-impossibility of weaning onto non-live foods. Consistent with SeriouslyFish data on water parameters and size.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — The Amazon Leaf Fish (Monocirrhus polyacanthus)
additional.notes: Tropical Fish Hobbyist profile. Detailed description of leaf-mimic morphology, hunting behaviour, blackwater origin, live-diet requirement, and breeding observations. Consistent with other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs deposited on flat surfaces; male guards clutch. Fry require live micro-foods (Artemia nauplii, micro-worms) from hatching. Breeding water must be very soft and acidic (pH 5.5–6.5). Rarely accomplished in home aquaria.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Live-feeder ONLY (refuses prepared foods reliably); ambush predator that mimics a drifting leaf. Requires steady supply of feeder fish/shrimp/insects. Single-fish species-only setup typical.
